title: Bending-like stress induced by solder joint under uniaxial tensile testing in 2G-HTS tapes: Impact and optimization approach
authors: Dean Liu, Yue Wu, Haoliang Xiang, Xiaofen Li, Caida Fu, Chiheng Dong, Yue Zhao
abstract: The reversible stress limit (\mathit{R}_{rev}) of second-generation high-temperature superconducting (2G-HTS) tapes is a critical performance indicator, typically characterized through uniaxial tensile testing. In practice, the accuracy of the measured Rrev value is often compromised by stress concentration induced by the voltage tap solder joint. The present study investigates the underlying interference mechanism using integrated experimental and numerical methods. Mechanistic analysis reveals that under uniaxial tensile loading, the local geometric inhomogeneity introduced by the solder joint induces an external, bending-like stress in the vicinity of the solder joint, transitioning from additional tensile stress in the zone adjacent to the joint to additional compressive stress in the zone remote from it. When the solder joint is attached to the front surface of the tape (the side closer to the superconducting layer), the superconducting layer experiences localized additional tensile stress, triggering premature damage and early \mathit{I}_{c} degradation. Consequently, an optimized back-surface soldering approach is proposed, which positions the superconducting layer in a localized compressive zone. Experimental validation demonstrates that the proposed approach effectively mitigates testing errors for various tape configurations. Notably, for the tape with a copper layer thickness of 5 μm, the measured \mathit{R}_{rev} increased from 546 MPa to 734 MPa, corresponding to a 42% increase, and moved closer to the actual value. The findings provide essential insights for the precision characterization of the electromechanical performances (EMPs) of 2G-HTS tapes.
summary: This work addresses the critical issue of inaccurate mechanical testing of high-temperature superconducting tapes due to stress concentrations from solder joints. By combining experimental measurements with FEM simulations, the authors prove that the joint induces damaging bending-like stresses. They propose and validate an optimized back-surface soldering technique, leading to significantly more accurate characterization of the tapes' true performance limits.
main_problem: The accuracy of measuring the reversible stress limit (Rrev) of 2G-HTS tapes is compromised by stress concentration induced by the voltage tap solder joint during uniaxial tensile testing.
main_result: Optimizing the soldering location to the back surface mitigates this stress interference, significantly increasing the measured Rrev (e.g., 42% increase for one tape configuration).
method: The study combines integrated experimental characterization (tensile testing, DIC, SHPM) with detailed numerical simulations using the Finite Element Method (FEM).
model_or_system: The physical system is a multi-layered 2G-HTS tape (REBCO) subjected to uniaxial tensile loading, with the primary focus on the mechanical stress state induced by the solder joint.
key_observables: Reversible stress limit (Rrev), critical current (Ic) degradation, local strain fields, and stress distribution maps.
important_parameters: Copper layer thickness ($5 \mu	ext{m}$ example), applied tensile stress, and the location of the solder joint (front vs. back surface).
main_assumptions: The FEM model simplifies the tape into a multi-layered composite structure, and the analysis assumes the stress concentration mechanism is dominated by geometric inhomogeneity.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison of Ic degradation and Rrev between front-surface and back-surface soldering approaches, alongside FEM stress maps demonstrating the induced bending-like stress profile.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the problem using front-surface testing results, then uses FEM and experimental diagnostics (DIC, SHPM) to mechanistically prove the existence of bending-like stress. Finally, it proposes and validates the back-surface soldering approach, showing quantitative improvements across multiple tape types.
why_interesting: While focused on materials science, the rigorous application of continuum mechanics (FEM) to model localized stress fields and their impact on a critical performance metric (Ic) demonstrates advanced multiscale modeling techniques applicable to complex quantum devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
